courses.subject: LING
courses.title: Invitation to Linguistics

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 404
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 404

courses.description:
(UGED1924 is double-coded with LING1000.)
This course invites students to explore the central components of human language, examining its structure and functions, how it is acquired, and how it changes over time. Students will have an overview of the grammatical properties of language, with respect to its sounds and sound system (phonetics and phonology), words and word formation (morphology), sentence patterns (syntax), and meaning (semantics). The course will highlight the psychological and biological foundations of language, and the applications of linguistics to other fields. Students will develop a basic understanding of language as a computational system that interacts with other cognitive systems of the human brain.

The course will also discuss the social aspects of language and the computational processing of human language. Students will become more sensitive to their own use of language, and develop an appreciation of recent advances in linguistic science, with respect to universals and particulars of language, language and society, language variation and change, language acquisition, as well as language, mind and the brain.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
LING1000 is double-coded with UGED1924.
Not for students who have taken UGED1924.
courses.course_syllabus:
Language, mind and nature

* Conceptions of language
* Design features of human language
* Relation of language and brain

Languages of the world

* Language diversity
* Living, endangered, and extinct languages
* Preservation of endangered languages
* Classification of languages and language families
* Pidgins and creoles

Phonetics and Phonology: The study of sounds and sound system

* Articulatory properties of different types of sounds
* Phonological rules and processes

Morphology: The study of words and word formation

* Classification of morphemes
* Different types of word formation processes

Syntax: The study of sentence patterns

* Concept of constituency
* Hierarchical structures of phrases and clauses in a sentence

Semantics: The study of meaning

* Nature of meaning
* Different types of sense relations

Language acquisition

* Developmental patterns and milestones in first language acquisition
* Characteristics and cross-linguistic influence in second language acquisition and bilingualism

Language variation and change

* Social factors underlying variation patterns
* Relation between language variations and language change

courses.subject: LING
courses.title: Exploring Grammar: Cantonese
courses.description: This course introduces the basic features of Cantonese grammar taking a primarily descriptive approach. The areas covered include Cantonese phonology, morphology, aspectual system, grammatical constructions and sentence final particles. Students should develop a sharpened sensitivity to the characteristics of Cantonese grammar and be able to appreciate its complexities and their own intuitions about the language.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken CHLL3162
courses.learning_outcomes:
- Have a better understanding of the value of the studies of Cantonese and its grammar in linguistics and multilingual societies
- Become familiar with common grammatical concepts and terms, with reference to the traditional grammatical framework and the generative framework
- Describe language phenomena and apply the grammatical concepts to analyzing the phonological, morphological and syntactic structures of Cantonese
- Become familiar with the salient grammatical features of Cantonese and the similarity and differences between Cantonese and Mandarin
courses.course_syllabus:
Topic

Contents/ Fundamental concepts

Linguistic status of Cantonese
Dialects vs. Languages, Cantonese as a Chinese dialect(?!), multi-lingual society, value of Cantonese in linguistics research

Phonology
Descriptive vs. prescriptive (正音), Cantonese phonological system, tones, phonotactic rules, dialectal variations, Jyutping

Morphology
Classification of morphological structure in traditional Chinese grammar, e.g. 偏正, 動補, 並列, etc., word categories, tagging

Topic comment structure
Contrast between subject- vs. topic-prominent languages (e.g. English and Cantonese), various constraints of topicalization

Sentence particles
Sentence particles vs phrase particles, semantics and pragmatics of particles

Nominals and Classifiers
Nominal structure, classifier types, (in)definiteness of nominals, differences between Cantonese and Mandarin

Verbal suffixes/ particles
Syntax and semantics of zo 咗, gwo 過, gan 緊, hoi 開, faan 翻, etc.

Ditransitive construction
Word order of ditransitive, Thematic roles, weight

Wh-interpretation
Various use of wh-words in Cantonese, e.g. wh-indefinite, negative wh-words, wh-placeholders, surprise wh¬-interpretation, etc.

Right Dislocation
Focus, syntax of sentence particles and implications to Chinese clausal structure

courses.subject: LING
courses.title: Exploring Grammar: English
courses.description: This course focuses on the grammatical structure of English and helps students explore the complexities of the structure of English sentences in a descriptive framework. Students will be exposed to a wide range of grammatical constructions and trained to apply the descriptive approach to analyse them. The training will enrich students’ understanding of the grammatical categories and structure of English while consolidating their foundational knowledge of the language. References are also made to common errors in students’ English to raise their awareness of the problems in learning English. The course will deepen students’ sensitivity toward the subtleties and complexities in the structure of English and enable them to improve their accuracy in the use of English.
courses.course_syllabus:
LING1003

| | Topic | Contents/ Fundamental concepts |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Descriptivism vs. prescriptivism | Descriptive vs. prescriptive grammar; spoken vs written; standard vs sub-standard; formality |
| Morphology | Morpheme; allomorphy; inflectional vs. derivational morphology; word formation (e.g. compounding, conversion, blending) |
| Tense and aspect | |
| Constituency and phrases | Basic concept of constituency; simple constituency tests (just enough to illustrate the idea) |
| Word classes and phrases | Major word classes (e.g. nouns; verbs; determiners; adjectives, adverbs, prepositions) and the corresponding phrases (e.g. NP, VP, and so on) |
| Clause structure, complements, adjuncts | subjects, objects, predicates; complement; adjuncts; expletive; agreement |
| Clause types | Interrogatives (*wh*-questions, *yes-no* questions, inversion, affix hopping and *do* support); exclamatives; imperatives; subjunctives; negative inversion |
| Subordination, coordination and relatives | Coordination; matrix vs. embedded clauses; noun clauses; adverb clauses; compound clauses; complex clauses; finite vs. nonfinite clauses; head word and gap; relative pronoun; restrictive vs. non-restrictive relatives |
| Rules | *There*-insertion; clefts and pseudo-clefts; dative movement; heavy-NP shift |
| X-bar syntax, complements and adjuncts | Introduction to the basic idea of X-bar syntax |
| Wh-movement | Wh-words and gap |
| Passives | Passive construction and its morphology; *by*-phrases; NP-movement |

courses.subject: LING
courses.title: Phonetics I
courses.description: This course introduces the study of sounds as an essential part of human language: how they are produced, and how they are perceived. Students will gain a solid understanding of articulatory mechanisms for speech sounds, become familiar with the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA), and acquire techniques of elicitation and transcription of speech sounds with special focus on English, Putonghua, and Cantonese.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Students can distinguish speech sounds using phonetic conventions.

2. Students will understand the articulatory and acoustic nature of speech sounds, and be introduced to a speech analysis software (Praat).

3. Students will be introduced to issues in speech production and perception research, and understand the complex nature of everyday speech communication.

4. Students will acquire the skills of producing and transcribing the sounds of the IPA symbols.

5. Students will have acquired the background knowledge for LING2004 Phonology I and LING3102 Phonetics II.
courses.course_syllabus:
LING2003

| | Topic | Contents/ Fundamental concepts |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Speech production mechanism | Different steps in the speech chain |
| Consonants | Place and manner of articulation, voicing and VOT, airstream mechanisms |
| Vowels | The vowel quadrilateral and its acoustic correlates |
| Suprasegmentals | Tones, stress, intonation, speech rhythm |
| Basic phonological concepts | Phonemes, allophones, features |
| Acoustic phonetics | Sound waves, formants, fundamental frequencies |
| Speech production | Coarticulation |
| Speech perception | Categorical perception, the McGurk effect |
| Transcriptions | Production and transcription of IPA symbols, and the Jyutping system for Cantonese |

courses.subject: LING
courses.title: Phonology I
courses.description: This course introduces core concepts of phonology, with a strong emphasis on data analysis from a wide variety of languages. How do sounds pattern together to form a phonological system in a particular language? What kinds of sounds are commonly found in human languages? Are there universal principles governing sound patterns in different languages? This course will also explore the theory of phonological representations, including distinctive features, syllables, and tone.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Pre-requisite: LING2003
courses.learning_outcomes:
* Understand basic concepts of segmental phonology, such as phonemes, minimum pairs, alternations
* Understand how segments are distributed within sound systems of different languages
* Understand how surface sounds are derived from underlying forms by applying phonological rules
* Be able to identify sound patterns and draw generalizations from different language data
* Be able to apply phonological concepts into data analysis
courses.course_syllabus:
Topic

Contents/ Fundamental concepts

Phoneme/ Allophone
Distributions/ alternations(near) minimal pairs/free variations

Phonemic inventories

Phonological processes
Assimulation/ Dissimilation/ Insertion/ Deletion/ Metatheses

Surface alternations
Underlying representations/ morphonemic alternations

Distinctive features
Definitions of distinctive Features/ Functions of Distinctive Features

Phonological Rules
Notations and formalism of phonological rules

Rule interaction
Rule ordering/ derivation from underlying forms to surface forms

courses.subject: LING
courses.title: Syntax I

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 402
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 402
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 402

courses.description: This course introduces some fundamental concepts in the study of syntax and looks at how the formation of phrases and sentences is principled and how syntactic rules are universal. These concepts will be introduced through grammatical structures accessible to the students, ranging from simple to complex constituents. The course enables students to see the capacity of the human linguistic system to form complex structures. Students will develop sensitivity toward natural language structures and gain hands-on experience of tackling syntactic problems in the form of a mini-project.
courses.course_syllabus:
LING2005

| | Topic | Contents/ Fundamental concepts |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Fundamentals in syntax | Grammar, syntax; competence vs. performance; I-languages vs. E-language; the language faculty; levels of adequacies in linguistic research; structural ambiguity; prescriptive vs. descriptive grammars; constituency structure and structural hierarchy |
| Structural relations | Recursiveness in syntax; diagnostics for constituency structures; coordination, embedding, adjunction, replacement, cleftings, pseudo-clefting, fronting, & movement; VP, TP, & CP structures; motivating X-bar/X' theory |
| Binding theory | Types of determiner phrases (DPs), anaphor, antecedent, reflexive, reciprocal & referential expression; difference between personal and reflexive pronouns; motivating structural dependencies and the Binding Theory: c-c-command, dominance & precedence relations |
| Empty categories & related theories | Verb/predicate argument structure, null/covert categories; wanna contraction & PRO; motivating Projection Principle and Full Interpretation; proposition, tensed clause; agreement; control; adverb placement |
| Head movement & VP structure | The VP-internal Subject hypothesis; V to T movement; lexical decomposition, motivating the VP Shell Hypothesis & light verb projection; V to v movement in Chinese as an illustration |
| A movement & related theories | Theta Theory & the Theta Criterion, theta grid & thematic hierarchy; external vs. internal arguments; argumental vs. non- argumental noun phrases, expletive; Case Theory, Case Filter; structural vs. lexical Cases; structural configuration of Case assignment; [A] csv [-A]/A-bar positions; Burzio’s Generalization & related constructions; passive, middle & unaccusative; stative vs. eventive sentences |
| Raising VS. Control and related theories | Difference between structural positions & arguments; raising construction; subject control vs. object control constructions; arbitrary PRO; exceptional Case marking construction |
| Wh-movement & related theories | Clause types and complementizers; Move-a, Xo (head) movement, XP (A & A’/A-bar movement); relative clause vs. appositive clause; movement and c-command |

courses.subject: LING
courses.title: Semantics

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 713
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 713

courses.description: How are expressions in a language system interpreted? What are the approaches towards decoding mechanisms in the study of meaning in natural languages? How does one account systematically for ambiguities in the meaning of sentences? To what extent is the study of meaning independent of language context and use? This course examines how meaning is systematically coded in the grammar of natural languages through structural and empirical methods of semantic analysis at the word, sentence and utterance levels. Basic concepts such as sense and reference, lexical semantics, truth functionality in conjunction, disjunction, implication and contradiction, events and entities, as well as quantification will be included.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Students who complete this course will:

• gain familiarity with a diverse range of semantic phenomena

• engage in the incremental construction of a formally explicit theory of truth-conditional semantics that captures these facts;
• have an opportunity to apply and possibly extend this theory towards the (further) description and analysis of a semantic phenomenon that particularly interests them.
courses.course_syllabus:
What is meaning?--some views on meaning, truth-condition, compositionality, entailment, contradiction, tautology

Sets and functions--set, relations between sets (subset, proper subset, superset), operation on sets (intersection, union, complement), ordered pair, function

Propositional logic--proposition, logical connectives, reasoning with truth tables, syntax and semantics of propositional logic

Predicate logic--Predicate (one-place predicate, two-place predicate) vocabulary of predicate logic (e.g. existential quantifier, universal quantifier) syntax and semantics of predicate logic , translations between predicate logic and natural language

Compositional semantics--type theory, composition rules: function application, predicate modification

Quantification--meaning of quantifiers, scope ambiguity,quantifier raising, type shifting

Polarity--entailment and monotonicity, negative polarity item

Inferences--presuppositions, defninte determiner implicatures and Gricean Maxims

Plural-- Commulativity, lattice structure

courses.subject: LING
courses.title: Linguistic Argumentation I
courses.description:
The course aims to train students in the articulation of ideas related to language issues. It will engage students in critical reflections on the character of linguistic units at various levels of grammar, as well as systematic observations and descriptions of language phenomena. Students will be introduced to the structure and norms of academic writing in the linguistic sciences, and will receive intensive practice in the speaking and writing techniques essential for the reporting and analysis of language data, in both English and Chinese.

Special Note: Please remove the obsolete course LING2001 (Linguistics Argumentation I) from the course exclusion.

courses.title: Linguistic Argumentation II
courses.description:
This course enhances students’ sensitivity to the structure of linguistic argumentation. Through critical discussion of selected cases, students will explore the ways in which one may arrive at generalizations based on linguistic evidence, how different analyses may apply to a given linguistic phenomenon, and the grounds on which one analysis may be judged to be superior to another. Standard arguments in linguistic analysis will be introduced with illustrations drawn from phonology, syntax and semantics. Concepts such as economy, internal consistency, pattern congruity, linguistically significant generalizations, and learnability will be highlighted.

Special Note: Please remove the obsolete course LING2002 (Linguistics Argumentation II) from the course exclusion.

courses.description:
(UGEC2925 is double-coded with LING2301.)
This course explores human language in the broader context of culture and society. How, for example, does one language community interacts with another language community in the multilingual world? Does language change and what are the processes involved? To what extent does the same language vary due to gender, power, status, and identity? How do men and women, people with higher and lower social status talk differently? How are these relationships reflected in the use of politeness markers and other linguistic devices? What are the implications of sociolinguistics on education and language planning?

Through introducing students to major issues in sociolinguistic studies, students will be able to develop a better understanding of how language functions in societal contexts from a linguistic perspective. Discussions will also focus on common sociolinguistic phenomena in daily communication, with a specific emphasis on the use of various languages and dialects spoken in Hong Kong. Exploring these topics will enhance students’ awareness of language as a cultural phenomenon and sharpen their sensitivity to the nuances of language use in the context of a multilingual world, with special reference to the sociolinguistic context of the Greater China area.

courses.course_syllabus:
Languages, dialects and varieties

* Language vs. dialect, language standardization, regional dialects, social dialects, styles, registers and genres

Multilingual societies and discourse

* Multilingualism, diglossia, code-switching, language shift, maintenance, and revitalization

Contact languages

* Lingua francas, pidgin and creole formation, linguistic characteristics of pidgins and creoles

Language variation and change

* Regional variation, linguistic variables, social variation, gender variation, language change

Language, gender and sexuality

* Sexist language, gender and sexuality identities

Sociolinguistics and education

* Social dialects and education, multilingual education, English as a global language

Language attitudes and identity

* Language attitudes and effects on identity; how identities are expressed through variations of language

Language policy and planning

* The goals of language policies, the role of government and language users, the roles of ethnic and national identities

courses.title: Comparative Grammar
courses.description: This course aims at introducing to students a number of major similarities and differences between Chinese and other languages of interest. The emphasis will be on the basic grammatical characteristics of these languages. The lectures and discussions are intended to increase students’ sensitivity toward linguistic forms through practice in comparative and contrastive analyses. Students are encouraged to investigate languages of their interest and explore the typological differences between these languages through independent or collaborative work with their peers.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Prerequisite: LING2005
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Enhance students’ sensitivity toward linguistic universals and variations.

2. Heighten students’ awareness of the linguistic universals and variations manifested in English, Mandarin and other languages of interest, and deepen their appreciation of a comparative approach to language study.

3. Gain basic analytic and research skills in comparative studies.
courses.course_syllabus:
LING3107

| | Topic | Contents/ Fundamental concepts |

| Introduction | - Comparative approaches to language study -The principles & parameters in syntactic theory -Universal Grammar |
| Nominal structure I: The DP hypothesis | - NP vs. DP - The DP hypothesis - The syntactic statuses of articles & demostratives |
| Nominal structure II: Nominal expressions in Mandarin & Cantonese | - Two competing analyses of nominal expressions in Mandarin & Cantonese: i) the DP hypothesis, and ii) the CIP hypothesis |
| Nominal structure III: Adjectives | - Three analyses of adjectives: i) adjectives as heads selecting NPs; ii) adjunction analysis; iii) adjectives as Specifiers of FPs |
| Nominal structure IV: Adjectival modification in Mandarin | - Attributive adjectival modification in Mandarin: Compunds or Phrases? |
| Wh-movement | - Wh-question formation in English - Wh-movement |
| Wh in-situ | - LF movement - Non-movement and unselective binding - Wh-indefinites |
| Passives | - Passivization in English - Long and short passives in Mandarin |
| Clausal structure I: The Spilt-CP hypothesis | - The structure of CP - Topic and focus - Operator vs. non-operator |
| Clausal structure II: Topic structures in Mandarin | - Definitions of topics - Classification of topic structures - Movement or base-generation |

courses.subject: LING
courses.title: First Language Acquisition
courses.description: This course introduces issues in the study of first language acquisition from a linguistic perspective. The topics covered include universal stages in first language acquisition, examining the child’s phonological, lexical and syntactic development and language socialization. Major theories of language acquisition and the critical period hypothesis will be examined and evaluated. While the primary focus of the course is on monolingual acquisition, issues in the study of children acquiring two first languages simultaneously will also be addressed.
courses.learning_outcomes: Students will gain an appreciation of innate and biological influences on language acquisition, and develop an understanding of the developmental milestones in first language acquisition. The course provides students with opportunities to conduct first-hand child language observation and data collection, and familiarizes students with some basic methods for analyzing child language data.
courses.course_syllabus:
LING3201

| | Topic | Contents/ Fundamental concepts |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Biological foundations of language | Innateness; learning by instinct; language faculty; critical period; maturation; selective language impairment. |
| The nature of language acquisition | Input; caretaker speech; positive and negative evidence; adult linguistic competence. |
| The nativist approach to language learning | Learnability; the argument from poverty of stimulus; Universal grammar; abstract linguistic rules and structures. |
| Empiricist approaches to language learning | Cognitive principles for language learning; limited scope formulas; usage-based models of language learning; verb islands and lexically based patterns; connectionist approaches to language acquisition. |
| Methods in the study of language acquisition | Naturalistic and experimental methods; methods for eliciting language production; methods for testing language comprehension. |
| Early lexical/semantic development | Word spurt; indeterminacy in children's word meanings; innate biases for learning the meanings of words; early noun bias in lexical development. |
| Morphological acquisition | The acquisition of (regular or irregular) morphological rules; overgeneralizations; constraints on word formation. |
| The acquisition of categories and clause structure | Criteria for determining the acquisition of syntactic categories; abstract categories vs. lexically-based patterns. |
| Further on the acquisition of categories and clause structure | The onset of functional categories in various languages; the continuity hypothesis in grammatical development; the development of inflection. |
| Pro-drop in child grammar | Parameter setting in language development; Pro-drop in child and adult languages. |
| Children's knowledge of syntactic constraints | Universal constraints on phrase structure, binding and anaphora. |
| Phonological acquisition | Jakobson's discontinuity theory of phonological development; phonological rules in children's speech; target language influence on infant speech perception and production. |
| Pragmatic development: The acquisition of definite/indefinite reference | Linguistic form, word order and the signaling of (in)definiteness; referential knowledge of speaker and hearer; thematic structure in narrative patterns. |

It examines the biological foundations of language and the nature of language competence from an evolutionary and developmental perspective. The ways in which language, thought and culture interact are explored. Emphasis will be placed on the principles and mechanisms that underlie speech perception, sentence processing and discourse comprehension, as well as structural factors in language production.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken PSYC3430
courses.course_syllabus:
Topic

Contents/ Fundamental concepts

unique features of natural languages
human languages versus animal communications

biological foundations of language
vocal tract structure, brain lateralization, aphasia, ‘language gene’

psychological mechanisms
memory, different types of processing

speech perception
categorical perception, the McGurk effect, models of speech perception

perception of written language
different writing systems, eye movements during reading, inner speech, models of word recognition

the mental lexicon
dimensions of word knowledge, organisation of the lexicon

utterance comprehension
garden path sentences, structural ambiguity

discourse comprehension

reference, coreference, genre, schema and discourse processing

language production
speech errors, linguistic plans

language and culture
language and thought, colour terms, taboos

courses.subject: LING
courses.title: Linguistics and Information Technology

meetings.location: William M W Mong Eng Bldg 712
William M W Mong Eng Bldg 712

courses.description: This course aims to introduce the applications of linguistics in the field of information technology. The focus of the course is on basic concepts in natural language processing, and ambiguities in human-machine interaction. Through hands-on experience, students will gain a better understanding of the problems with current speech recognition software and natural language processing systems, and of how linguistic knowledge can help provide solutions to some of these problems.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken TRAN2610
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Students will be exposed to major topics in the field. The emphasis will be on how linguistic knowledge can augment computational methods in natural language processing tasks. They will be given non-technical introduction to the basic idea/rationale behind the computational algorithms. Students will be able to appreciate the synergy of the field of Linguistics and Computer Science.

2. Students will have a good understanding of the data-oriented methodology, e.g. the use of corpus data, training, testing, and evaluation.

3. Apart from learning the concepts, students will learn to use some user-friendly tools to do some simple text processing and analysis themselves.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction--It provides a general introduction to the significance of IT on linguistics and vice versa. An overview of natural language processing research and practical everyday IT applications will be provided. The strengths and limitations of computers and its implications to language processing will be discussed.

2. Tokenization, stemming and word segmentation--Text processing begins with tokenization of text chunks. While it looks like an easy task, it is a non-trivial task for computers. Special attention will be given to the Chinese word segmentation problem where word boundaries are not explicitly indicated in the Chinese writing system.

3. Part-of-speech (POS) tagging--POS tagging is a task that enhances the information of the text and critically affects further text processing. POS tagging schemes will be reviewed. The bigram approach to tagging will be demonstrated.

4. Corpora-based training and testing--In the last 20 years, the field of natural language processing is dominated by the corpus-oriented approach in all sorts of tasks. This is also an area where linguists’ contribution is important. We will demonstrate how corpora can be used to train and test word segmenter and POS tagger.

5. Tag Cloud / Search Engine -- The bag-of-words model will be introduced as the general representation of text documents. The generic representation enables computers to quantify the relative “importance” of words in texts so that keywords or chunks can be selected for retrieval.

6. Email Spam Filtering / Sentiment Analysis--Despite their superficially very different functions, both applications can be considered to be particular examples of document classification. We will discuss how corpus-based machine learning methods make use of keyword in documents to classify documents into different categories.

7. Speech recognition--Students will be introduced to the basic principle behind the mapping of speech signals to words. Concepts include formant, spectrum, Fourier transformation, etc. Statistical strategy (just the idea, not the math) to recover words from phones will be examined.
courses.feedback_evaluation:
Students are encouraged to talk to the instructor or the TA through email or contact hours. Class website will be used for information sharing and discussion.

Mid-term course evaluation will be conducted to enable the teacher to adjust the teaching style and content accordingly.

3. Jurafsky, Daniel and James H. Martin. (2000) Speech and Language Processing. (1st Ed.) Prentice Hall.

courses.title: Special Topics in Language and Mind
courses.description: This course introduces students to one or two particular aspects of cognitive and neuroscientific research into language and mind, with in-depth teaching into a special topic(s) in the broad area of psycholinguistics and neurolinguistics, such as language comprehension, language production, language learning, language disorder, neurolinguistics, bilingualism, experimental methodology, statistical analyses, and so on and so forth. Topics will vary from year to year. Students will be able to use experimental methods in investigating linguistic problems and apply linguistics knowledge into the analysis of language-related phenomena in the areas of language and mind. Offerings with different subtitles can be taken up for credit, up to a maximum of two times.

courses.subject: LING
courses.title: Foundations I: Phonetics and Phonology
courses.description: This course introduces students to a unified approach to language as a complex structure represented in the minds of its speakers. Empirical linguistic data will be drawn across languages to enable students to understand the intimate relation between language and the human mind. On the basis of this understanding, students are led to explore the core areas of linguistics. The exploration starts with natural language sound systems and phonological components of grammar. These will be explained with basic concepts and recent theoretical advances in linguistic studies alongside new findings in language acquisition. Students will learn to apply these concepts and ideas to tackle linguistic problems.

courses.subject: LING
courses.title: Foundations II: Syntax and Semantics
courses.description: This course is taught concurrently with LING5101 Foundations I: Phonetics and Phonology, aiming at introducing students to the core areas of linguistics. Taking a modular approach to the language system, this course examines the morphological, semantic and syntactic components of the grammar, as well as the interaction among them. The lectures are intended to provide students with a solid grounding in basic linguistic concepts, which will enable them to tackle linguistic problems, and formulate their own analyses to prepare them for further studies in the discipline. A wide range of data will be discussed to develop students’ sensitivity toward linguistic phenomena with systematic properties in form, meaning and structure.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Linguistics or MA Chi Linguis & Language Acq or PgD in Communication Disorders and Sciences or MSc Speech-Language Pathology
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Develop sensitivity toward language phenomena with systematic properties in form, meaning and structure.
2. Attain an understanding that individual languages are specific realizations of Universal Grammar.
3. Build foundations in syntax (e.g. constituent structure and basic syntactic operations motivated by morphology and semantics) and semantics (e.g. predicate-argument, reference, tense~aspect, proposition), and their interfaces (through morphology), to pave the way for further studies in the program.
4. Gain basic problem solving and analytical skills in the above areas to facilitate studies in more advanced courses.

courses.subject: LING
courses.title: Foundations in Language Acquisition
courses.description: The acquisition of first language by children has been considered a remarkable feat. How do children accomplish this feat so rapidly and effortlessly? What are the stages they go through in mastering the different aspects of language? What does the development of language in children tell us about the human language faculty? These questions will be examined in light of modern linguistic theory, and nativist and interactionist accounts will be compared. Topics in second language acquisition will also be covered. Questions such as how interlanguage grammars develop in adults and the role of the mother tongue in the construction of interlanguage grammar will be addressed.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Linguistics or MA Chi Linguis & Language Acq or PgD in Communication Disorders and Sciences or MSc Speech-Language Pathology
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing the course, students will achieve some fundamental understanding of:

1. The nature of language and its acquisition by humans and primates.
2. The central issues involved in language acquisition inclusive of nature versus nurture, language attainment, cross-linguistic influence, linguistic evidence and the nature of input, as well as linguistic processing.
3. Language phenomena under exceptional circumstances involving language disorder and language attrition.

courses.description: This course provides an introduction to a relatively new area of linguistic exploration: sign language as a natural language system. We will lead students into a variety of disciplinary studies that adopt sign language as a focus of research. Examples of these disciplines are linguistics, language in education, language and brain, language and cognition, language development, language and society, and language and culture. The course aims to tackle these issues in light of the current developments in sign language research and see how they shed light on our understanding of deaf issues.

Different theoretical perspectives on second language acquisition will be reviewed and methodologies will be surveyed. Data will be drawn mainly from English and Chinese as target languages.

courses.subject: LING
courses.title: Topics in Bilingualism
courses.description: This course discusses general issues in the study of bilingualism from the linguistic perspective. Basic questions such as how to define and measure bilingualism, degrees of bilingualism, and types of bilinguals will be examined. Psycholinguistic and sociolinguistic aspects of bilingualism at the individual and societal levels will be covered within the larger context of language contact. Issues in bilingualism such as code-mixing, medium of instruction, bilingual education and language policy for bilingual society will be treated with special relevance to the Hong Kong context.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Linguistics or MA Chi Linguis & Language Acq
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Acquire the basic concepts in the field of bilingualism
2. Compare and contrast language development in bilingual and monolingual children
3. Deepen students’ appreciation of the significance of bilingualism in the individual and society
4. Raise students’ multilingual awareness in the age of globalisation
5. Analyze bilingual data and evaluate the bilingual situation in the local community
6. Develop ability to learn, discuss and work in a group

It starts with a general introduction on the acquisition of sign language as a first language by deaf children, covering the developmental milestones in phonology, morphology and syntax. The second part of the course will explore how deaf children acquire spoken language in the context of bilingual acquisition and spoken language literacy development. No prior knowledge of a sign language is required.

courses.subject: LING
courses.title: Sign Linguistics
courses.description: This course provides a general introduction to the linguistic analysis of phonology, morphology and syntax in sign languages. It aims to demonstrate that the system of organization in sign language grammar reflects natural language properties and that sign language grammar is as complex and rule-governed as spoken languages. Focus will be placed on how the visual modality, availability of paired manual articulators, non-manual features as well as the use of signing space affect the organization of grammar at various linguistic levels. No prior knowledge of a sign language is required.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Linguistics and MA Chi Linguis & Language Acq
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completion of this course, students will be able to:
1. Given a sign,
a. Identify the five parameters of the sign.
b. State how common the form of each parameter is in the world’s sign languages.
c. Transcribe each of the five parameters using Stokoe Notation.
d. Transcribe each of the five parameters using modified Stokoe Notation.
e. Discuss the theoretical implications of the symbols they have chosen.
2. Distinguish phonetics and phonemics as they apply to sign languages,
3. Solve phonemic problems using sign language data,
4. Distinguish between classical phonemics and generative phonology,
5. Solve problems in generative phonology using sign language data,
6. Discuss the differences in explanatory power between classic phonemics and generative phonology,
7. Apply the concepts of phonological universals and markedness to 1 and 2 finger handshapes in sign languages,
8. Given a group of related signs,
a. Identify the formational processes that distinguish the signs.
b. Provide an explanation for the occurrence of the formational process.
c. Identify if the formational process is likely to result from historical change.
d. Arrange any historical changes in the likely order they occurred.
e. Discuss the implications of the formational process for linguistic analysis and language teaching.
9. Apply the concept of “syllable” as it relates to sign language data,
10. Provide examples of phonotactic rules in sign languages,
11. Provide examples of suprasegmentals in sign languages.
12. Coherently explain to a linguist what they have learned in this course.
13. Understand the morphological processes in sign languages
14. Understand the interaction between morphology, syntax and semantics in sign languages
15. Able to formulate their own analysis on tackle the linguistic problems
16. Able carry out critical evaluation on linguistic theories
17. Able to develop detailed research skills in different areas of sign linguistic enquiry.

courses.subject: LING
courses.title: Topics in Sociolinguistics
courses.description: This course explores human language in the broader context of culture and society. How does language relate to culture and world-view? How does language interact with social structure, gender and individual identity? To what extent do men and women talk differently? How does language reflect relations of power and status between the speakers? The use of pronouns, politeness markers and other linguistic features will be examined. Exploration of these topics aims to enhance students’ awareness of language as a cultural phenomenon and sharpen their sensitivity toward the nuances of language use in relation to cultural complexities, with special reference to Hong Kong culture.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA Linguistics or MA Chi Linguis & Language Acq
courses.learning_outcomes:
This subject will enable students to specify societal and linguistic concerns with reference to the social functions of language. In focus is the description of language behaviours in the context of situations which the students of this subject find typical and noteworthy. Upon completion of this subject the students will be able to:
1. Design and conduct a domain analysis
2. Describe social situations by conducting an ethnographic microanalysis
3. Identify sociolinguistic variables in Chinese and English
4. Analyze sociolinguistic variables

Students are allowed to take this course more than once, and gain the units each time they pass the course. However, students cannot take the course under the same topic twice.

courses.subject: LING
courses.title: Topics in Psycholinguistics
courses.description:
This course studies language as a cognitive system which interfaces with other subsystems of mind. Emphasis will be placed on linguistic properties rather than psychological mechanisms or various techniques in psycholinguistic studies. The principles and mechanisms that underlie speech perception, sentence processing and discourse comprehension, as well as structural factors in language production form the focus of this course. This course first examines the unique features of human languages and the biological foundations of language. It then explores the perception of various linguistic aspects (speech sounds, words, sentences and discourse) before touching on issues in language production. Major models of language production and perception will be introduced. After examining different aspects of our language competence, this course concludes with a discussion of language and culture. However, issues related to the origin of language and language acquisition will not be covered in this course.
Students are expected to be familiar with the basic concepts in phonetics, phonology, morphology and syntax.

courses.subject: LING
courses.title: Language Disorders
courses.description: This survey course introduces non-clinical students to fundamental concepts of language disorders in pediatric and adult populations. Characteristics of primary language impairment, aphasia, dysarthria, and hearing impairments, as well as articulation, fluency, and voice and other related disorders affecting language are among the topics to be discussed. Diagnostic techniques and treatment strategies are also introduced. Research studies in language disorders will be reviewed.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MA in Linguistics or MA in Chinese Linguistics and Language Acquisition
courses.learning_outcomes:
At the end of the course, students will be able to
 Describe the fundamentals of language and communication, and basic characteristics of language disorders
 Explain the etiology of different language disorders
 Analyze patient signs and symptoms and attribute them to specific language disorder types
 Search and summarize clinical research evidence

courses.description: The course aims to train students in conducting linguistic research. General research methodology will be introduced, with a focus on methods commonly used in linguistic research and analysis. Students may be required to take part in field trips and expeditions.

2. Students will be able to use electronic resources to facilitate their research.
3. Students will produce a research proposal on a topic of their choice.
4. Students will know how to assess research hypotheses and methods critically.
5. Students will understand the importance of statistics in data analysis; they will also have a basic idea of some common statistical tests.
6. Students will learn how to communicate their ideas formally in writing and in oral presentation.

courses.subject: LING
courses.title: Phonological Theory
courses.description: This course aims to introduce the development of phonological theory over the past half century and to provide a critical survey of the current issues in phonological research. Topics include segmental alternation, syllable structure, tone, stress, and prosodic effect in word formation. Students will gain a solid understanding of the characteristics of different phonological frameworks over the course of the development of phonological theory, and will learn how to analyze a variety of language data within different phonological frameworks.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MPhil-PhD Linguistics, MPhil Linguistics or PhD Linguistics
courses.learning_outcomes: This course allows students to attain a solid understanding of different phonological frameworks over the course of the development of phonological theory. Students will be familiar with the characteristics of different phonological frameworks, and be able to analyze a variety of language data using these frameworks.

courses.subject: LING
courses.title: Syntactic Theory
courses.description: This course provides students with a concise and critical introduction to the central issues and perennial problems in syntactic theory, with special focus on the Government and Binding Theory and the Minimalist Program. Through exercises, class discussions, and presentations, students will gain a solid understanding of the concepts and principles which have been of central significance in the recent development of syntactic theory. Whenever relevant, data from Mandarin and other languages will be used to motivate and instantiate the analyses that pertain to the central issues in syntactic theory.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MPhil-PhD Linguistics, MPhil Linguistics or PhD Linguistics
courses.learning_outcomes: This course allows students to attain a good understanding of the key concepts and principles that are of central significance to the recent development in syntactic theory and enhances their sensitivity toward the central issues and problems that arise in the recent development. Students will be equipped with the analytical skills for analyzing data and tackling syntactic problems, and be trained to identify and articulate empirical and theoretical issues in the literature.

courses.subject: LING
courses.title: Topics in Semantics
courses.description: This course aims at exploring various semantic phenomena and examining the relation between meaning and structure in human languages. Emphasis will be given to current topics that are central to research in semantics. The discussion will also help students appreciate the relation between semantics and other sub-fields such as pragmatics, syntax and philosophy of language. The study of the related literature will help student develop the ability to carry out semantic analysis.

courses.learning_outcomes: Students will learn about the fundamental issues of important semantic topics and the different theoretical approaches to analyzing them. The course will help students appreciate the interface issues between semantics and other Linguistics sub-fields. Students will also become familiar with how semantic analysis is conducted.

courses.subject: LING
courses.title: Topics in Neurolinguistics & Psycholinguistics
courses.description: This course introduces students to selected topics concerning neurobiology of language as well as language representation, processing and production. Major theoretical debates and contemporary issues that address different aspects of language will be discussed. The selected topics vary from term to term, but may include syntactic and phonological priming as evidence for language representation, computational modeling of language phenomena, neural correlates of first and second language acquisition, explanatory neurolinguistics, comparative studies of language processing, models of speech production, lexical tone processing, cross-modal studies of language processing, and the bilingual brain.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MPhil-PhD Linguistics, MPhil Linguistics or PhD Linguistics
courses.learning_outcomes:
(Vary from term to term, but may include the outcomes below)

At the end of the course, students will be able to

 Identify major structures in the brain, especially those that are relevant to language

 Describe research methods used in psycholinguistics and neurolinguistics research

 Discuss how language representation, processing, and production functions are enabled by the brain

 Analyze research studies in psycholinguistics and neurolinguistics
courses.course_syllabus:
Content, highlighting fundamental concepts (vary from term to term; some examples are highlighted)

Topics may include:

Neuroanatomy

Psycholinguistics as an empirical science

Research methods

Language components in the brain

Language acquisition and the brain

Models of language processing

Models of language production

The bilingual brain

Lexical tone processing

Contents/fundamental concepts may include:

Major systems of the brain

Aims of psycholinguistics; empirical research design, psychological methods, hypothesis testing

Lesion and neuroimaging methods, word recognition experiments, structural priming, eye tracking

Associations between brain systems and language structures

Neural consequences of first and second language acquisition

Computational models of language such as the TRACE model

Theoretical models of speech production such as the DIVA model

Code switching, cognitive control network in the brain

Cortical and subcortical circuits subserving lexical tones

courses.description: This course is a continuation of LING 6980 (Research Methodology). Selected experimental methods will be discussed in depth. Topics may include EEG, fMRI, eye-tracking, advanced behavioral methods and data analytics and computational mathematics. Students will learn hands-on techniques and will complete a research project using the methods introduced.

courses.subject: LING
courses.title: Quantitative Methods in Language Research
courses.description: This course presents an introduction to research designs and data analyses that are commonly used in experimental linguistic research. The course will emphasize on practical skills in data handling and analyses, in particularly using R. The course first introduces students to experimental designs as a prerequisite to statistics. Then it progresses from data distribution and descriptive statistics to gradually more complicated statistical techniques that are commonly used to analyze linguistic data (e.g., correlation, t-test, non-parametric tests, ANOVA, regression). With a focus on both conceptual understanding and practical skills in statistics, the course offers both lectures and also practical sessions where students have hands-on experience using R to analyze actual language data.
courses.enrollment_requirement: For students in MPhil-PhD Linguistics, MPhil Linguistics or PhD Linguistics
courses.learning_outcomes:
* Students will learn how to interpret statistical results in experimental reports on language and related areas.
* Students will have a good understanding of experimental designs and their implications for statistical analyses.
* Students will develop critical quantitative thinking in their empirical research.
* Students will have conceptual understanding of common statistical techniques in language research and the actual skills to apply them to collected data.
* Students will be able to use R to conduct simple data analyses.
courses.course_syllabus:
| **Topic** | **Contents/fundamental concepts** |
| --- | --- |
| Research design in experimental linguistics | Correlational design, cause-effect design, cross-sectional design, within/between-subject design |
| R as a statistical platform | R for handling data, R for statistical analyses, R for making graphs |
| Descriptive statistics | Mean, mode, median, z-score, standard deviation |
| Correlation | The relation between two datasets |
| t-test | How to use parametric test to compare two means |
| Non-parametric tests | How to use non-parametric tests to compare means |
| ANOVA | How to compare multiple groups |
| Regression | How to model a set of observations using independent predictors |
courses.feedback_evaluation: Students are encouraged to give feedback or comments on course contents and teaching materials throughout the course. Students can contact either the lecturer or tutor directly. See contact details below. Mandatory term-end evaluation for teacher’s reflection will also be conducted.
